name: Slaughter-Brute
role: Expendable Melee Swarm / Scrap Stack Fuel
physical: Two meters tall, nearly as wide. Fusion of engineered muscle and crude cybernetics. Each individual unit is unique — hydraulic piston forearm, optical sensor over a missing eye, steel jaw welded in place. Scrap armor: mismatched plates, chain links, a car hood for a chestplate. Weapons: sharpened hull-plate cleavers, engine-piston maces, pipe-bomb slug-throwers that fire once with a thunderclap then require fist-hammered reloading.
narrative: Most numerous, most expendable. Workers and scrap-pickers given a weapon and pointed at the enemy. Tactical doctrine: 'run toward the loud noises.' Yet terrifying — ten Brutes charging, howling, swinging industrial cutting tools with engineered-laborer strength. +25% melee from faction passive. Every death — their own or the enemy's — generates Scrap Stacks, fuelling survivors. The Brutes are fuel. They know it. A Veteran Brute who survived five battles and REFUSED to be disposable — that's the story the Horde tells around forge fires.
scrapNote: PRIMARY Scrap Stack generator. Default count: 6 per side (MVP). Each Slaughter-Brute death within 150px of a surviving Horde unit triggers one stack.

name: Scrap-Trukk
physical: Open-topped four-wheeler welded from three salvaged chassis. Exposed engine belching smoke, running on anything flammable. Flat bed with welded railing for 8 standing Brutes. Driver behind salvaged plate-glass windshield. Pintle-mounted slug-thrower on the cab roof spraying inaccurate fire. Covered in welded spikes, trophies, crude graffiti. FAST — recklessly, dangerously fast. Sheds parts mid-drive. Driver compensates through skill and complete disregard for physics.
narrative: One purpose: deliver Brutes to the enemy's face. Roars at arrest-worthy speeds, bouncing over terrain, crashing through fences. In emergencies, the Trukk IS the weapon — ramming crushes infantry, flips light vehicles, embeds in walls. Driver and passengers often survive the impact and continue fighting on foot among the wreckage.

name: Loota-Mech
physical: Bipedal walker assembled from four or more factions' salvaged tech. Gutted Terran walker chassis. Revenant legs rewired with crude bypass circuits. Unity Accord railgun on right arm, Terran flamethrower on left, Eternal Shards energy coil duct-taped to the top as 'targeting' — connects to nothing but glows impressively. Cockpit: a rat's nest of wiring, toggle switches, and handwritten labels. Mek-Boy pilot controls via joysticks, foot pedals, and percussive maintenance.
narrative: Most versatile and most unpredictable. No two identical. Might have excellent ranged capability (if the railgun works) or melee only (if the railgun jammed and the Mek-Boy rerouted power to a hydraulic fist). Loadout reflects recent salvage — fighting Terran produces ballistic mechs; fighting Shards produces psychic coils connected to car batteries.

name: Gore-Roller
physical: Enormous multi-ton vehicle dominated by a 3m-diameter spiked iron roller. Chain-driven by a monstrous engine. Reinforced cab: driver plus two side gunners. Rear engine belches black smoke, backfires with concussive BANG. No ranged primary. The weapon IS the roller — drives OVER targets. Infantry pulped. Vehicles flattened. Fortifications crushed. Continuous grinding metallic ROAR as roller turns.
narrative: How do you breach without explosives? Drive a building-sized spiked iron drum through the front door. No tactical subtlety. No flanking. No suppression. Drives forward in a straight line — everything in that line ceases to exist. Against heavy armor: roller bounces off. Primary value: breaching tool and terror weapon — the psychological impact often breaks morale before contact is even made.

name: War-God Idol
physical: Twelve meters, six-legged. Central pillar: siege tower plus mobile shrine plus weapons platform. Body: grotesque scrap-metal face — leering idol with semi-religious significance. Eyes: forge-fires behind iron grates. Mouth: industrial shears with hydraulic bite force. Six legs: repurposed mining drills. Festooned with weapons operated by 30+ crew climbing over the exterior. Atop: Waaagh!-Caller broadcasting psycho-sonic war cry through amplified speakers.
narrative: Not just a weapon — a SYMBOL. Walking monument to violence and the Horde's refusal to die quietly. +15% damage aura from the Waaagh!-Caller within 30m. Mass pushes through walls, crushes barricades. Crew repairs mid-battle with hammers and torches. Its destruction is a campaign event — forge-fire eyes gutter, structure collapses with seismic impact. Veterans fight with desperate fury (+20% dmg for 30s). Rookies waver (−10% speed for 30s).

name: Dakka-Copter
physical: Crude rotary-wing built from salvaged helicopter parts. Oversized engines making it faster than any engineer would sanction. Armament: as many slug-throwers as the airframe can carry without falling. Pilot fires ALL simultaneously — a cone of inaccurate, devastating suppressive fire scything through infantry formations.
narrative: The name is onomatopoeia — the sound when firing: 'DAKKA-DAKKA-DAKKA-DAKKA.' Pilots scream the word in time with their guns. No doctrine. No precision. Just an entire airframe's worth of lead at once.

name: Scrap-Bommer
physical: Lumbering slow aircraft with massive bomb bays filled with literal garbage: scrap metal, engine blocks, rusted iron beams, occasionally live explosives. Targeting system: bombardier looking out a floor hatch saying 'now.' Payload drops — ground cratered by tons of falling scrap.
narrative: Inaccurate, destructive, and deeply satisfying for the crew. The enemy has learned to fear the sight of slow-moving Horde aircraft. Doesn't matter what's in the bays — it weighs a lot and it's falling.

name: Kamikaze-Missile
physical: A piloted rocket. Single volunteer in a crude cockpit bolted to a massive unguided rocket motor. Steers via joystick and raw courage. Upon impact: warhead detonates with devastating force. The pilot does not survive.
narrative: The ultimate expression of Horde philosophy — choosing to become the weapon. Single-use: produced, launched, consumed in one engagement. The Horde's answer to heavily armored targets that nothing else can crack. Volunteers are never in short supply.

name: Rust-Zeppelin
physical: Massive gas-filled airship with welded scrap-metal gondola. Absurdly large, painfully slow, impossible to miss. Value: cargo capacity — transports an entire company of Brutes plus Trukks. Deploys via rear loading ramp. Vulnerable to sustained anti-air — burst fire punctures the gas envelope.
narrative: Slow fiery descent is terrifying for the enemy and hilarious for Horde passengers who leap from the crashing Zeppelin into enemy positions below. The crash is considered an acceptable delivery method.

name: Sky-Barge
physical: Enormous flat-bottomed anti-gravity platform (looted Shards gravity tech wired to a diesel generator). Deck covered in weapons: artillery, rockets, slug-throwers, and a crane arm that GRABS enemy vehicles, lifts them, and drops them. Slow, barely maneuverable, aggressively ugly.
narrative: Hovering weapons platform that saturates defenses from directly above, bypassing cover and fortifications entirely. The crane is not technically a primary weapon. It is always used as one.

name: Slag-Fighter
physical: Tiny, crude, barely spaceworthy. Cockpit pod bolted to an oversized engine. Minimal weapons: single nose cannon. Almost no armor. Advantage: cost — produced faster than any other faction's interceptors.
narrative: Overwhelm through sheer numbers. Flood space with cheap, expendable craft that swamp enemy point defense. Each Slag-Fighter death within range contributes to Scrap Stacks aboard surviving ships.

name: Flak-Corvette
physical: Small warship bristling with rapid-fire slug-throwers optimized for fast-moving targets. Fills space with a continuous stream of physical projectiles — a zone of shrapnel shredding fighters and intercepting torpedoes.
narrative: Crude but effective — the Horde's answer to the Terran Aegis-Frigate. Physical kinetic projectiles are highly effective against Core Guardians and cannot be deflected by energy shields.

name: Ram-Ship
physical: Built around one philosophy: hit the enemy WITH the ship. Massive 50m reinforced iron battering ram bow. Oversized engines for impossible acceleration. Shock-absorbing interior. Attack run: max speed, magnetic grapple locks on target hull, SLAMS battering ram into the enemy flank. 25% self-damage per successful ram. Deploys boarding parties through the breach.
narrative: The impact breaches the enemy hull. Hundreds of Horde warriors pour through into corridors — close-quarters melee where the +25% melee bonus makes them dominant. Conventional naval doctrine has no answer for a 200,000-ton ship that simply rams you.

name: Tether-Barge
physical: Large slow vessel built around a magnetic tractor beam array and hull-mounted processing facility. Collects battlefield debris during space combat — destroyed components, ejected cargo, disabled ships — processes into raw Scrap in real-time during battle.
narrative: The space equivalent of Wreckage Salvage: converts every death into resources. A fleet with a Tether-Barge sustains operations far longer. Every destroyed ship — friend or foe — feeds the Barge's processors. The Horde makes war pay for itself.

name: Asteroid-Fortress
physical: 800m hollowed-out asteroid with engines bolted to the back and weapons on every surface. Internal forges, barracks, fighting pits, Warlord's throne room. Crew 2,000+. Armor IS the asteroid — hundreds of meters of rock absorbing everything with geological indifference. Dozens of captured weapon systems, internal Slag-Fighter bays, boarding craft, and ramming capability.
narrative: The thesis statement: we took a rock from the void, hollowed it out, filled it with guns and fury, and now it's the most dangerous object in the galaxy. 'Da Big Gun' fires once per battle — 50% of target's max HP regardless of armor type. Recoils the Fortress backward 200m. The Horde considers this a feature.
